answers.value:
Maple Crest Engineering Inc. is a mid-sized civil and structural engineering consulting firm specializing in municipal infrastructure projects, light commercial developments, and small-scale industrial facilities. The company provides end-to-end services including preliminary feasibility studies, detailed design, drafting, construction administration, and project management. Typical projects include road and intersection improvements, small bridges and culverts, stormwater management systems, low- to mid-rise commercial buildings, and tenant fit-outs for office and light industrial spaces.

The firm employs a mix of licensed professional engineers, EITs (engineers-in-training), CAD technologists, and project coordinators. Most work is performed from the main office with periodic on-site visits for inspections, progress meetings, and quality control during construction. Field work is generally low to moderate risk and involves visual inspections, basic surveying, and site meetings with contractors and municipal officials. Employees are provided with safety training and appropriate PPE (high-visibility vests, hard hats, safety boots, etc.) when visiting construction sites.

Primary clients are municipalities in Ontario, small to medium private developers, local architects, and manufacturing companies requiring plant layout modifications or structural assessments. The business operates in a professional office environment with standard business hours, robust use of computer-aided design software (AutoCAD, Civil 3D, Revit), and frequent collaboration with external stakeholders. The company has a stable revenue base from repeat municipal contracts and long-term relationships with local developers and architects.

answers.value:
The company employs a small number of contract and seasonal staff to handle workload peaks during the construction season (typically April through November). In 2025, they anticipate engaging 2–3 contract CAD technologists and 1 part-time field inspector through a technical staffing agency.

Contract CAD technologists generally work 25–35 hours per week for 4–6 month periods, assisting with drafting detailed design drawings, updating as-built plans, and preparing permit submission packages. These individuals typically work on-site at the office under the supervision of a senior technologist or project engineer, and they use company-provided workstations and software.

The seasonal field inspector is usually hired from May through October to support site visits for road reconstruction and subdivision servicing projects. This person spends a majority of time on outdoor job sites conducting visual inspections, taking photos, and documenting construction progress. They receive the same safety orientation, PPE, and WSIB coverage as permanent staff. Seasonal staff are not eligible for the full group benefits package but may receive limited health and dental coverage if their contract exceeds six months, subject to insurer eligibility rules.

answers.value:
Although the ownership is family-based (the company founder, Daniel Fraser, is the father of the current President, Laura Fraser), less than 50% of the total employees are related. Out of 23 total employees, 3 are related by family:

1. Laura Fraser (President) – daughter of the founder (semi-retired and not on the benefits plan).
2. Michael Fraser (Senior Structural Engineer) – Laura’s cousin.
3. Erin Fraser (HR & Office Coordinator) – Laura’s sister-in-law.

These three family members do not all live in the same household. Laura lives with her spouse and children at a different address, Michael lives separately with his own family, and Erin resides in another part of the city. Their roles and compensation are aligned with market benchmarks and are documented in job descriptions and performance reviews. No other employees are related by blood or marriage, and hiring decisions are made through a standardized recruitment process to avoid nepotism concerns.

answers.value: Complications related to a high-risk pregnancy leading to physician-advised bed rest and subsequent maternity leave. Initially placed on short-term disability due to severe gestational hypertension and risk of preeclampsia, later transitioned to maternity/parental leave.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Samantha’s prognosis is good. Her OB-GYN anticipates full recovery postpartum with no long-term physical limitations. She is expected to remain off work through the birth and immediate postnatal period, with a tentative return-to-work date in mid-September 2025 on a gradual schedule (e.g., starting at 60% hours for the first month). She is currently able to perform limited remote administrative tasks (e.g., answering occasional project questions by email) but is not expected to take on billable project responsibilities until she returns from full leave.

answers.value: Non-work-related lower back injury, including a herniated lumbar disc, sustained while lifting heavy furniture at home. The injury has resulted in chronic lower back pain, reduced mobility, and difficulty sitting for extended periods, which directly impacts his ability to perform prolonged computer-based drafting work.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Carlos is under the care of a physiotherapist and a specialist in physical medicine. The current medical opinion is that he is likely to make a partial to full recovery within 6–9 months from the date of injury, provided he adheres to his treatment plan (physiotherapy twice weekly, core strengthening, and modified daily activities). A gradual return-to-work plan is being developed, likely starting with 2–3 hours of seated work per day in an ergonomic workstation setup and increasing as tolerated. He may require permanent ergonomic accommodations and the ability to alternate between sitting and standing to prevent recurrence.

answers.value: A life waiver of premium application was submitted through the current group insurer in January 2025 due to the anticipated length of his disability. As of the latest update, the insurer has acknowledged receipt and requested additional medical documentation from his specialist. Final approval is still pending, and premiums for his life coverage are currently being paid as usual until a decision is made.

answers.value:
The requested quote is not a simple duplication of the current plan; several intentional changes are being sought to better align coverage with employee needs, manage premium costs, and address feedback from staff surveys conducted in late 2024.

Key differences from the current plan include:

1. Extended Health Care (EHC):
   - Increasing paramedical coverage (physiotherapy, massage therapy, chiropractic, and psychology/counselling) from $500 per practitioner per year to $750 combined per year, with an 80% reimbursement level. This change is driven by higher utilization of physiotherapy and counselling services, particularly by employees dealing with ergonomic and stress-related concerns.
   - Introducing coverage for virtual healthcare/telemedicine consultations, which the current carrier only partially supports. Employees have requested more flexible access to care, especially during busy project phases when it is difficult to attend in-person appointments.

2. Prescription Drugs:
   - Maintaining the 80% reimbursement level but introducing a mandatory generic substitution provision (unless a physician indicates “no substitution”) to help control rising drug costs. The current plan reimburses brand-name medications by default, which has contributed to premium increases at renewal.
   - Adding a preferred pharmacy network option to encourage cost-effective dispensing fees while still allowing employees to use their existing pharmacies if they choose.

3. Dental Benefits:
   - Adjusting dental coverage from 100% basic / 60% major to 90% basic / 60% major, with the goal of moderating overall dental premium costs while still providing strong coverage for preventive and restorative services. Orthodontic coverage will remain limited to dependents only, with a modest lifetime maximum.

4. Disability Coverage:
   - Aligning the long-term disability (LTD) elimination period from 120 days to 90 days to reduce the gap between the end of short-term disability and the start of LTD benefits. This change is based on the experience of current and recent claims (e.g., a back injury case and a high-risk pregnancy) where employees expressed concern about potential income gaps.
   - Ensuring that LTD benefits remain employee-paid so that any benefits received are non-taxable. This structure is already in place with the current carrier but will be explicitly maintained in the new quote.

5. Life and AD&D:
   - Maintaining the current life and AD&D benefit levels but requesting more flexible options for optional (employee-paid) life coverage to give staff the ability to purchase additional protection for their families without going to an individual market.

Overall, the requested changes are intended to provide more meaningful coverage in high-use areas (paramedicals, disability coordination, and telemedicine), while introducing modest cost-containment measures (generic substitution, slightly lower basic dental reimbursement) to keep the plan sustainable over the long term.
